items.techniques: Dressmaking, Dart Sewing, Concealed Zip Insertion, Fusible Interfacing Application, Faux Leather Sewing, Pocket Flap Construction, Waistband Construction, Facing Attachment, Edge Stitching, Topstitching, Hemming, Pressing

steps.title: Cut the Pattern Pieces
steps.content: Cut one front skirt piece, one back skirt piece on the fold, one pair of side panels, one pair of pocket flaps from vegan leather, and one waistband piece. Cut the matching interfacing pieces for the front facings and waistband sections.

tip.text: Double-check that the side panels are cut as mirror images before removing the pattern pieces.

steps.title: Sew the Front Darts
steps.content: Transfer the dart markings onto the front skirt and stitch the darts. Press them toward the center of the skirt as indicated in the pattern.

tip.text: Tie off thread tails at the dart points instead of backstitching to prevent puckering.

steps.title: Apply Fusible Interfacing
steps.content: Fuse the lightweight interfacing to the designated facing and waistband pieces. Press carefully using a pressing cloth to avoid distortion.

tip.text: Allow the interfaced pieces to cool flat before moving them to ensure the adhesive sets properly.

steps.title: Prepare the Pocket Flaps
steps.content: Fold and press the faux leather pocket flap pieces according to the pattern markings. Topstitch around the visible edges to create a neat finish.

tip.text: Use a slightly longer stitch length when sewing faux leather to achieve cleaner topstitching.

steps.title: Attach the Pocket Flaps
steps.content: Position the faux leather pocket flaps onto the front skirt section at the marked locations and stitch securely in place.

tip.text: Check both pocket flaps are positioned at exactly the same height before stitching.

steps.title: Join the Side Panels
steps.content: Sew the side panels to the front skirt piece, matching all notches carefully. Press the seam allowances open.

tip.text: Grade seam allowances where woven fabric meets faux leather to reduce bulk.

steps.title: Insert the Concealed Zip
steps.content: Install the 12 inch concealed zip into the center back opening using a concealed zipper foot. Stitch the remainder of the center back seam below the zip.

tip.text: Press the zipper coils flat before sewing to help create a truly concealed finish.

steps.title: Assemble the Skirt Body
steps.content: Place the front and back skirt sections right sides together and stitch the side seams. Finish the seam allowances and press open.

tip.text: Make sure the hip and waist seams align accurately before sewing.

steps.title: Construct the Waistband
steps.content: Join the waistband pieces and apply any remaining interfacing. Press the waistband in preparation for attachment.

tip.text: Use plenty of steam when shaping the waistband for a crisp professional appearance.

steps.title: Attach the Waistband
steps.content: Sew the waistband to the skirt waistline, matching side seams and notches. Turn the inner edge under and secure neatly.

tip.text: Avoid stretching the waist edge while stitching or pressing.

steps.title: Finish the Hem
steps.content: Turn up the hem allowance, press thoroughly, and stitch in place to complete the pencil skirt silhouette.

tip.text: Hand stitch the hem for the most invisible finish on dress skirts.

steps.title: Attach the Buttons and Final Press
steps.content: Sew the six buttons onto the skirt as shown in the design and give the entire garment a final press. Check that the zip operates smoothly and the pocket flaps lie flat.

tip.text: Mark button placement carefully before sewing to keep the decorative details evenly spaced.

items.name: Daisy Pocket Skirt
items.materialNeeded:
59 inches barkcloth fabric (55 inches wide)

items.techniques: Dressmaking, Pocket Construction, Pocket Flap Construction, Pleat Sewing, Waistband Construction, Interfacing Application, Button Attachment, Side Seam Sewing, Hemming, Topstitching, Pressing

steps.title: Cut Out the Pattern Pieces
steps.content: Cut the skirt front and back pieces, pocket bag lining pieces, pocket bag facings, pocket flaps, waistband pieces, and interfacing according to the pattern layout. Transfer all notches and markings.

tip.text: Be sure to cut mirrored pocket pieces correctly to avoid mismatched pockets.

steps.title: Prepare the Pocket Openings
steps.content: Position the pocket bag facings over the pocket opening areas on the skirt front. Stitch around the opening lines, clip carefully, and turn the facings through to the wrong side. Press the pocket openings neatly.

tip.text: Clip accurately into the corners without cutting through the stitching line.

steps.title: Construct the Pocket Bags
steps.content: Attach the pocket bag lining pieces to the prepared openings and stitch around the curved pocket edges. Press the pocket bags toward the inside of the skirt.

tip.text: Understitch close to the pocket opening seam to keep the lining from rolling outward.

steps.title: Create the Front Pleats
steps.content: Transfer the pleat markings onto the skirt front. Fold and baste the pleats in place, matching the marked lines and pressing them sharply.

tip.text: Check that both pleats mirror each other before securing them permanently.

steps.title: Make and Attach the Pocket Flaps
steps.content: Interface the pocket flap pieces, sew right sides together, trim corners, turn right side out, and press. Position each flap above the pocket openings and stitch securely in place.

tip.text: Use a point turner to achieve crisp flap corners.

steps.title: Join Front and Back Skirt Sections
steps.content: Place the front and back skirt sections right sides together and sew the side seams. Finish seam allowances and press open.

tip.text: Keep pocket bags clear of the seam line when stitching side seams.

steps.title: Assemble the Waistband
steps.content: Apply interfacing to the waistband pieces. Sew the waistband sections together as required and press the seam allowances open.

tip.text: Shape the waistband slightly with steam for a smoother fit around the waist.

steps.title: Attach the Waistband
steps.content: Pin the waistband to the skirt waistline, matching notches and side seams. Stitch, grade the seam allowances, and press the waistband upward.

tip.text: Avoid stretching the waist edge while attaching the waistband.

steps.title: Finish the Waistband Interior
steps.content: Fold the inner waistband edge under and hand stitch or stitch-in-the-ditch to secure the inside neatly.

tip.text: Hand finishing the waistband gives the cleanest professional result.

steps.title: Create the Button Closures
steps.content: Mark the button placement on the pocket flaps and sew the two buttons securely in place as decorative accents.

tip.text: Check button alignment on both pockets before stitching permanently.

steps.title: Hem the Skirt
steps.content: Turn up the hem allowance, press thoroughly, and stitch or hand sew the hem according to your preferred finish.

tip.text: Allow the skirt to hang for several hours before hemming if your fabric has any bias sections.

steps.title: Final Press and Finishing
steps.content: Give the entire skirt a final press, flatten the pleats, shape the pockets, and check that all seams, flaps, and buttons are secure.

tip.text: Do not over-press textured barkcloth fabric or you may reduce its characteristic texture.

items.name: Meadow Wrap Skirt
items.materialNeeded:
55 inches fabric

items.techniques: Dressmaking, Pleat Sewing, Waistband Construction, Fusible Interfacing Application, Side Seam Sewing, Button Loop Construction, Covered Button Technique, Hemming, Gathering, Pressing

steps.title: Cut the Fabric Pieces
steps.content: Cut one pair of front skirt pieces from fabric, one pair from interfacing, one back skirt piece cut on the fold, and one waistband piece cut on the fold from fabric and interfacing. Transfer all markings and notches.

tip.text: Ensure the front skirt pieces are mirrored correctly before cutting.

steps.title: Apply Interfacing
steps.content: Fuse the lightweight interfacing to the wrong side of the front skirt sections and waistband piece according to the pattern instructions.

tip.text: Allow interfaced pieces to cool flat before handling to prevent distortion.

steps.title: Form the Front Pleats
steps.content: Match the pleat markings on each front skirt section and stitch the pleats into place. Press the pleats toward the centre front and baste across the waist edge to secure them.

tip.text: Uneven pleats will affect the drape and symmetry of the finished skirt.

steps.title: Attach the Side Seams
steps.content: Place the skirt front and back sections right sides together and sew both side seams. Finish the raw edges and press the seams open.

tip.text: Press each seam immediately after stitching for a crisp professional finish.

steps.title: Prepare the Waistband
steps.content: Sew the waistband ends together as instructed. Fold the waistband lengthwise and press. Match the centre markings before attaching.

tip.text: Check that the waistband seam aligns with the skirt seam before stitching.

steps.title: Attach the Waistband
steps.content: Pin the waistband to the skirt waistline, matching centres and side seams. Stitch in place and grade the seam allowances before pressing upward.

tip.text: Trim seam bulk inside the waistband to help it lie flat.

steps.title: Create the Button Loop
steps.content: Make a narrow fabric loop and position it at the waistband opening as indicated on the pattern. Secure firmly into the waistband seam.

tip.text: Test the loop size against your button before permanently stitching it.

steps.title: Finish the Waistband Interior
steps.content: Fold the inner waistband edge to the inside and stitch in the ditch or hand sew neatly to enclose all seam allowances.

tip.text: Hand stitching gives the cleanest finish on lightweight fabrics.

steps.title: Hem the Skirt
steps.content: Turn up the hem allowance, press thoroughly, and stitch the hem in place according to the pattern instructions.

tip.text: Allow the skirt to hang before hemming if the fabric has any bias movement.

steps.title: Make the Covered Buttons
steps.content: Create two covered buttons using matching fabric. Gather the fabric around the button forms and secure tightly according to the button kit instructions.

tip.text: Pull the fabric evenly around the button to avoid wrinkles on the front surface.

steps.title: Attach the Buttons
steps.content: Sew the covered buttons onto the waistband and front overlap positions corresponding to the button loops and closure markings.

tip.text: Use doubled thread and several passes through each button for durability.

steps.title: Final Press
steps.content: Give the skirt a final press, ensuring the pleats fall smoothly and the waistband sits flat. Check that both buttons and loops are secure.

tip.text: Avoid flattening the pleats with excessive steam after they have been shaped.

items.name: Willow Denim Button Skirt
items.materialNeeded:

items.techniques: Dressmaking, Denim Sewing, In-Seam Pocket Construction, Pocket Facing Application, Waistband Construction, Fusible Interfacing Application, Buttonhole Sewing, Button Attachment, Topstitching, Hemming, Pressing

steps.title: Cut the Pattern Pieces
steps.content: Cut one skirt back piece on the fold, two skirt front pieces, one waistband on the fold, two side front pocket pieces, and two pocket facings. Transfer all notches and markings accurately.

tip.text: Mark the pocket placement notches clearly before removing pattern pieces.

steps.title: Prepare the Pocket Openings
steps.content: With right sides together, pin the pocket facings to the front skirt sections. Stitch following the marked pocket opening line, clip curves, and turn the facings to the inside. Press carefully.

tip.text: Clip curves close to the seam without cutting through the stitching.

steps.title: Attach the Pocket Pieces
steps.content: Position the side front pocket pieces behind the pocket openings and stitch around the pocket bags. Secure the top edges within the seam allowance.

tip.text: Topstitch close to the pocket opening for a crisp denim finish.

steps.title: Join Front and Back Skirt Sections
steps.content: Place the front skirt sections and back skirt section right sides together. Sew the side seams, matching notches and pocket edges. Finish seam allowances and press toward the back.

tip.text: Ensure the pocket bags are fully enclosed and not caught in the side seam.

steps.title: Apply Interfacing
steps.content: Fuse interfacing to the wrong side of both front skirt sections along the opening edge and to the waistband piece.

tip.text: Use a pressing cloth when fusing interfacing to denim to prevent shine marks.

steps.title: Construct the Front Closure
steps.content: Fold and press the front opening extensions according to the pattern markings. Create the overlap and underlap sections that will hold the buttonholes and buttons.

tip.text: Check the overlap direction before stitching because it cannot easily be corrected later.

steps.title: Attach the Waistband
steps.content: Fold the waistband in half and stitch the short ends. Match notches and sew the waistband to the skirt waistline. Trim seam allowances and press toward the waistband.

tip.text: Grade seam allowances inside the waistband to reduce bulk.

steps.title: Finish the Waistband Interior
steps.content: Turn under the remaining waistband edge and stitch in place from the right side, enclosing the waist seam neatly inside.

tip.text: Keep the waistband edge even all the way around for a professional finish.

steps.title: Create the Buttonholes
steps.content: Mark the buttonhole positions along the front overlap. Sew buttonholes through the interfaced section of the skirt.

tip.text: Test buttonhole settings on a denim scrap before sewing on the garment.

steps.title: Hem the Skirt
steps.content: Turn up the hem allowance to the desired length, press thoroughly, and stitch the hem in place.

tip.text: Use a denim needle and slightly longer stitch length when hemming thick fabric.

steps.title: Attach the Buttons
steps.content: Position and sew seven buttons onto the front closure, matching the buttonhole placements.

tip.text: Check closure alignment with all buttonholes before attaching the final button.

steps.title: Final Press and Finishing
steps.content: Press the skirt thoroughly, shape the pocket openings, flatten the waistband, and verify that the button closure functions smoothly.

tip.text: Use plenty of steam and a clapper when pressing denim for the sharpest finish.

items.name: Harper Button Skirt
items.materialNeeded:
59 inches fabric

10 inches fusible interfacing
4 buttons (1 inch / 25mm)
8 inches concealed zipper
Matching sewing thread
Dressmaking pins or clips

Iron and pressing cloth
items.techniques: Dressmaking, Panel Construction, Lining Construction, Concealed Zip Insertion, Waist Facing Construction, Fusible Interfacing Application, Understitching, Slip Stitching, Hemming, Button Attachment, Topstitching, Pressing

steps.title: Assemble the Outer Skirt Panels
steps.content: With right sides together, sew the side panels to each side of the front and back centre panels. Finish the seam allowances and press them toward the side panels. Topstitch close to the seam lines on the skirt panels.

tip.text: Topstitching after pressing helps the panel seams stay crisp and professional.

steps.title: Construct the Lining Panels
steps.content: Repeat the same panel construction process for the lining pieces, joining the side panels to the centre front and centre back lining sections.

tip.text: Keep the lining pieces separate from the outer skirt pieces to avoid accidentally mixing panel sets.

steps.title: Insert the Concealed Zipper
steps.content: Position the concealed zipper into the left side seam of the outer skirt and stitch in place. Sew the remaining section of the seam below the zipper and press the seam open.

tip.text: Use a concealed zipper foot to achieve an invisible finish and avoid sewing too far from the zipper teeth.

steps.title: Sew the Remaining Side Seam
steps.content: With right sides together, stitch the opposite side seam of the skirt and press the seam allowances open.

tip.text: Pressing seams open reduces bulk and improves the fit of the skirt.

steps.title: Construct the Lining Side Seams
steps.content: Sew the side seams of the lining. On the zipper side, leave the opening above the zipper marker unstitched. Press all seam allowances open.

tip.text: Do not sew across the zipper opening area or the lining will prevent the zipper from functioning properly.

steps.title: Attach the Lining to the Skirt
steps.content: Pin the lining to the skirt at the waist edge, matching centre front, centre back and all panel seams. Stitch the layers together around the waistline.

tip.text: Match all seam intersections carefully to keep the panel lines aligned.

steps.title: Finish the Zipper Area
steps.content: Slip-stitch the folded lining edge neatly around the concealed zipper tape and secure the lining close to the zipper opening.

tip.text: Keep stitches away from the zipper teeth to prevent the zipper from catching.

steps.title: Prepare the Waist Facings
steps.content: Apply fusible interfacing to the waist facing pieces. Sew the facing sections together, press the seams open, and finish the lower edge with an overlock or zigzag stitch.

tip.text: Allow interfaced pieces to cool completely before handling to maintain shape.

steps.title: Attach the Waist Facing
steps.content: With right sides together, pin the waist facing to the skirt waistline. Stitch around the waist edge, clip curves where necessary, trim seam allowances, and understitch the facing.

tip.text: Understitching is important to keep the facing from rolling to the outside.

steps.title: Turn and Secure the Facing
steps.content: Fold the facing to the inside of the skirt and press the waist edge. Fold under the facing ends at the zipper opening and slip-stitch them in place. Secure the lower edge of the facing to the lining.

tip.text: Hand stitching creates a cleaner finish than visible machine stitching in this area.

steps.title: Hem the Skirt and Lining
steps.content: Mark the desired finished length. Turn up and stitch the skirt hem. Trim the lining shorter than the skirt, create a double-fold hem on the lining, and stitch securely.

tip.text: Ensure the lining remains shorter than the outer skirt so it does not show when worn.

steps.title: Attach the Buttons
steps.content: Sew the four decorative buttons onto the skirt front according to the pattern markings, taking care not to stitch through the lining.

tip.text: Check button alignment before sewing because they are a prominent design feature.

items.name: Autumn Pocket Skirt

items.techniques: Dressmaking, Dart Sewing, Zipper Insertion, Pocket Flap Construction, Waistband Construction, Fusible Interfacing Application, Topstitching, Edge Stitching, Hemming, Hook and Bar Fastening Attachment, Button Attachment, Pressing

steps.title: Prepare and Sew the Back Skirt
steps.content: Cut all pattern pieces according to the cutting guide. Neaten the side and centre-back edges of the back skirt pieces. Fold and stitch the back darts, pressing them toward the centre back. Join the back skirt pieces along the centre-back seam with right sides together.

tip.text: Press darts over a tailor's ham to maintain the skirt's shaping.

steps.title: Insert the Zipper
steps.content: Sew the centre-back seam from the split notch to the zipper notch. Press the seam allowances open and install the zipper into the opening. Stitch securely and press.

tip.text: Check zipper alignment before final stitching to prevent a twisted centre-back seam.

steps.title: Construct the Front Skirt
steps.content: Neaten the side edges of the front skirt. Join the centre-front edges together and stitch. Finish the seam allowances and press the seam toward the left side. Add a second row of stitching approximately 0.2 inches from the seam.

tip.text: Uneven topstitching on the front seam is highly visible, so use a guide on your machine if available.

steps.title: Make the Pocket Flaps
steps.content: Place each pair of flap pieces right sides together and stitch around the pointed edges, leaving the top edge open. Trim seam allowances, turn right side out, shape the corners and press flat.

tip.text: Trim corners diagonally before turning to achieve crisp flap points.

steps.title: Attach the Pocket Flaps
steps.content: Position the finished flaps along the waistline at the marked notches on the skirt front and baste them in place.

tip.text: Check that both flaps sit at the same height before stitching.

steps.title: Join Front and Back Skirt Sections
steps.content: Match the front and back skirt pieces at the side seams. Stitch the seams, then press the seam allowances open.

tip.text: Press each seam immediately after sewing for a smoother finished garment.

steps.title: Prepare the Waistband
steps.content: Fuse interfacing to the waistband piece. Press one long edge under by 0.6 inches. Position the raw edge of the waistband along the skirt waistline and stitch in place, ensuring the overlap extensions are positioned correctly.

tip.text: Double-check the waistband overlap lengths before sewing, as they affect the closure fit.

steps.title: Finish and Topstitch the Waistband
steps.content: Fold the waistband ends inward by 0.6 inches. Fold the waistband over the waist seam and press. Edge-stitch one end and continue stitching along the waistband, adding a second decorative row approximately 0.2 inches away.

tip.text: Use an edge-stitching foot to keep parallel topstitching lines evenly spaced.

steps.title: Finish the Hem and Back Split
steps.content: Neaten the lower edge of the skirt and the split edges. Turn up the hem by 1 inch and press. Stitch the hem and split in place, then add a second row of stitching approximately 0.2 inches from the first.

tip.text: Ensure the split edges remain symmetrical before hemming.

steps.title: Attach Closures and Buttons
steps.content: Hand-sew the hook and bar fastening to the waistband ends. Attach the two buttons securely to the pocket flaps to complete the skirt.

tip.text: Reinforce button stitching because the flaps are a focal design feature.

items.techniques: Dressmaking, Gathered Skirt Construction, Concealed Zipper Insertion, Waistband Construction, Fusible Interfacing Application, Stitch-in-the-Ditch Sewing, Hemming, Hand Sewing, Hook and Bar Fastening Attachment, Seam Finishing, Pressing

steps.title: Prepare the Fabric Pieces
steps.content: Cut the front and back skirt pieces on the fold along with the waistband pieces. Fuse interfacing to the waistband. Neaten one long edge of the waistband and the side edges of both skirt pieces.

tip.text: Mark the waistband extension clearly before sewing to avoid attaching it to the wrong side.

steps.title: Sew the Side Seams
steps.content: Place the skirt pieces right sides together and sew the side seams. Stitch one side seam completely and leave an opening at the top of the remaining side seam for the zipper.

tip.text: Do not accidentally close the zipper opening when sewing the second side seam.

steps.title: Insert the Concealed Zipper
steps.content: Press the seam allowances open and install the concealed zipper into the prepared opening using a concealed zipper foot or zipper foot.

tip.text: Check that the waistband seam lines align across the zipper before final stitching.

steps.title: Attach the Waistband
steps.content: Place the waistband along the waist edge with right sides together and stitch in place, leaving the waistband extension protruding from the right-hand side as indicated.

tip.text: Staystitch the waist edge first if your fabric frays easily or stretches.

steps.title: Finish the Waistband Ends
steps.content: Fold the waistband in half lengthwise and stitch the left-hand end and extension. Trim bulk from the corners, turn right side out and press neatly.

tip.text: Trim seam allowances diagonally at the corners for sharper points.

steps.title: Turn and Secure the Waistband
steps.content: Turn the waistband to the inside of the skirt, enclosing the waist seam. Pin carefully and align the folded inner edge over the waist seam line.

tip.text: Uneven folding can cause the waistband width to vary around the skirt.

steps.title: Stitch in the Ditch
steps.content: From the right side of the garment, stitch in the ditch along the waistband seam to secure the inner folded edge invisibly.

tip.text: Use matching thread and sew slowly to keep the stitching hidden within the seam.

steps.title: Finish the Hem
steps.content: Neaten the lower edge of the skirt. Turn up a 0.8 inch hem, press thoroughly and secure using hand hemming stitches or a herringbone stitch.

tip.text: Allow the skirt to hang before hemming if using a fabric with any bias sections.

steps.title: Attach the Closure
steps.content: Sew the hook and bar fastening to the waistband ends directly above the zipper to complete the skirt.

tip.text: Position the hook and bar before sewing to ensure the waistband overlaps correctly.

items.name: Daisy Pleated Skirt
items.materialNeeded:
98 inches fabric
1.2 inches wide elastic
Matching sewing thread

items.techniques: Dressmaking, Inverted Pleat Construction, Patch Pocket Construction, Elastic Waistband Channel Construction, Belt Loop Construction, Tie Belt Construction, Edge Stitching, Topstitching, Hemming, Pressing

steps.title: Draft and Cut the Pattern Pieces
steps.content: Download and print the diagram, then draft the pattern pieces. Cut the front and back skirt pieces on the fold, two pocket pieces, two belt tie strips and four belt loop rectangles according to the measurements provided.

tip.text: Check all drafted measurements twice before cutting because this is a no-pattern project.

steps.title: Prepare the Pocket Openings
steps.content: Neaten the long edges of the pocket pieces. Fold and press 0.4 inches, then 1.6 inches along the pocket tops. Fold the pocket tops to the right side and stitch each side edge using a 0.4 inch seam allowance.

tip.text: Use a pressing gauge to keep pocket folds perfectly consistent.

steps.title: Finish and Attach the Pockets
steps.content: Trim corner bulk, turn the pocket tops to the wrong side and press. Fold and press the remaining pocket edges. Edge-stitch the pocket tops, then position the pockets approximately 2 inches from the side edges and 12 inches above the hem. Pin and edge-stitch in place.

tip.text: Ensure both pockets are positioned symmetrically before stitching permanently.

steps.title: Create the Inverted Pleats
steps.content: Form three inverted pleats at the top of both the front and back skirt pieces, one in the centre and one on each side. Stitch approximately 3.5 inches down the centre of each pleat and press flat.

tip.text: Baste pleats first and verify the finished waist measurement before permanent stitching.

steps.title: Join Front and Back Skirt Pieces
steps.content: Place the front and back skirt sections right sides together and sew the side seams. Press the seam allowances open.

tip.text: Do not stretch the fabric while sewing the side seams or the skirt may hang unevenly.

steps.title: Create the Elastic Waist Channel
steps.content: At the top edge of the skirt, fold and press 0.4 inches, then fold and press 3.1 inches to the wrong side. Edge-stitch the channel in place, leaving a small opening. Sew a second row of stitching approximately 1.4 inches above the first to create the elastic casing.

tip.text: Keep the channel width consistent so the elastic feeds smoothly.

steps.title: Make the Belt Loops
steps.content: Sew the short ends of each belt loop rectangle together, turn right side out and press. Fold the raw edges inward and position the loops over the pleats at the skirt sides. Stitch above and below the waistband channel.

tip.text: Reinforce belt loop stitching because they will take strain from the tie belt.

steps.title: Insert the Elastic
steps.content: Cut a length of elastic to fit comfortably around the waist plus extra overlap. Thread it through the waistband channel with a safety pin, overlap the ends and stitch securely. Pull the elastic inside the casing and close the opening.

tip.text: Check that the elastic has not twisted inside the channel before closing the gap.

steps.title: Hem the Skirt
steps.content: Fold and press 0.4 inches, then 1.6 inches around the lower edge of the skirt. Pin and edge-stitch the hem in place.

tip.text: Allow the skirt to hang overnight before hemming if using a drapey fabric.

steps.title: Construct the Tie Belt
steps.content: Join the two belt strips across the short edges and press the seam open. Fold the strip in half lengthwise with right sides together.

tip.text: Match the strip widths accurately at the joining seam for a professional finish.

steps.title: Sew and Turn the Belt
steps.content: Angle both ends of the belt and stitch along the ends and long edge using a 0.4 inch seam allowance, leaving a turning gap. Turn right side out, shape the points, press thoroughly and slip-stitch the opening closed.

tip.text: Trim seam allowances at the angled ends before turning to reduce bulk.

items.name: Meadow Tulip Skirt

items.techniques: Dressmaking, Tulip Wrap Skirt Construction, Pleat Formation, Stretch Fabric Sewing, Waistband Construction, Elastic Waistband Insertion, Binding Application, Stretch Stitching, Hemming Web Application, Hemming, Seam Finishing, Pressing

steps.title: Cut the Pattern Pieces
steps.content: Cut one pair of front skirt pieces, one back skirt piece on the fold, one waistband and two binding strips measuring approximately 1.2 inches by 32.3 inches. Transfer all pleat markings to the fabric.

tip.text: Mark pleats accurately before removing pattern pieces to ensure both sides match.

steps.title: Create the Front Pleats
steps.content: Fold three pleats at the top of each front skirt piece. Bring the outer edges of each pleat together and fold through the centre so the pleats point toward the side seam on the right side. Stitch across the top of the pleats to secure.

tip.text: Baste pleats first and compare both front sections before permanent stitching.

steps.title: Prepare and Attach the Binding
steps.content: Fold the binding strips in half lengthwise and pin along the curved wrap edges without stretching. Stitch using a 0.4 inch seam allowance, trim and neaten close to the stitching line, then press the binding away from the skirt edge.

tip.text: Do not stretch the binding while sewing or the wrap edge may ripple.

steps.title: Form the Tulip Front
steps.content: Overlap the right front skirt section over the left front section to create the tulip wrap shape. Pin across the waist edge and stitch to hold the overlap in position.

tip.text: Check the overlap direction carefully before stitching because reversing it changes the design.

steps.title: Attach the Back Skirt
steps.content: Neaten the lower edge of the back skirt. Place the back skirt over the front section with right sides together. Stitch both side seams using a stretch stitch, then neaten the seam allowances.

tip.text: Use a narrow zigzag or stretch stitch to preserve fabric flexibility.

steps.title: Construct the Waistband
steps.content: Join the short ends of the waistband with a 0.6 inch seam allowance, leaving an opening for elastic insertion. Fold the waistband in half lengthwise with wrong sides together.

tip.text: Leave a sufficiently large opening for threading the elastic later.

steps.title: Attach the Waistband
steps.content: Match the waistband seam to the centre back and pin the waistband to the waist edge. Stretch the waistband slightly to fit and sew using a stretch stitch. Finish the seam allowances.

tip.text: Distribute the fabric evenly around the waistband before stitching.

steps.title: Insert the Elastic
steps.content: Cut elastic to fit comfortably around the waist plus extra overlap. Thread it through the waistband casing with a safety pin, overlap the ends and stitch securely. Pull the elastic fully into the waistband.

tip.text: Ensure the elastic remains flat and untwisted inside the waistband.

steps.title: Close the Waistband Opening
steps.content: Hand slip-stitch the opening in the waistband closed after the elastic has been secured.

tip.text: Use matching thread and small stitches to make the closure nearly invisible.

steps.title: Hem the Skirt
steps.content: Fold and press a 1.2 inch hem along the lower edge of the back skirt. Apply fusible hemming web and machine stitch in place so the hemline aligns with the bound front wrap edges.

tip.text: Check hem alignment carefully because the back hem must match the front wrap edge length visually.

items.name: Rosie A-Line Skirt
items.materialNeeded:
47 inches fabric (44 inches wide)
30 inches contrast fabric
Curved Petersham ribbon
9 inches concealed zipper
Matching sewing thread
Dressmaking pins or clips

items.techniques: Dressmaking, A-Line Skirt Construction, Pocket Construction, Dart Sewing, Concealed Zipper Insertion, Petersham Waist Finish, Edge Stitching, Understitching, Seam Finishing, Hemming, Hand Sewing, Pressing

steps.title: Cut and Prepare the Pocket Pieces
steps.content: Cut the front skirt, back skirt and contrast pocket pieces. Fold the top pocket facings along the fold line and stitch the corner nearest the centre front on both pockets. Trim corner bulk and turn the facings to the wrong side.

tip.text: Trim seam allowances diagonally at the corners to create crisp pocket edges after turning.

steps.title: Understitch the Pocket Facings
steps.content: Lengthen the stitch length slightly and sew a row of understitching approximately 0.6 inches from the cut edge. Pull the stitching line gently so the pocket edge rolls neatly to the inside. Press thoroughly.

tip.text: Skipping pressing at this stage can cause pocket edges to roll outward later.

steps.title: Attach the Pockets
steps.content: Position the pockets on the front skirt according to the pattern notches. Pin in place and edge-stitch around the sides and lower edge, leaving the pocket opening free. Neaten the pocket edges together with the skirt side seams.

tip.text: Verify both pockets are mirrored and positioned evenly before stitching.

steps.title: Sew the Back Darts
steps.content: Transfer dart markings and stitch the darts on the back skirt. Press the darts toward the centre back.

tip.text: Tie thread tails at the dart tips instead of backstitching to avoid puckering.

steps.title: Construct the Skirt Body
steps.content: Sew the centre-back seam from the hem up to the zipper opening mark. Reinforce the top of the seam. Join the front and back skirt sections at the side seams and press all seam allowances open.

tip.text: Do not accidentally stitch into the zipper opening area.

steps.title: Insert the Concealed Zipper
steps.content: Press the seam allowances at the zipper opening. Position the concealed zipper face down with the teeth aligned to the crease line and stitch in place using a concealed zipper foot or regular zipper foot.

tip.text: Check that the waistline aligns perfectly across both sides of the zipper before final stitching.

steps.title: Finish the Centre Back Opening
steps.content: Secure the upper ends of the zipper and test the closure. Pull the zipper slider through and close the opening. Press the centre-back seam neatly.

tip.text: Press around the zipper using a pressing cloth to avoid damaging the zipper tape.

steps.title: Apply the Petersham Waist Finish
steps.content: Stitch the inside edge of the curved Petersham ribbon to the waist edge of the skirt. Fold the longer outer edge over the seam allowance and edge-stitch from the right side to secure.

tip.text: Keep the Petersham smooth and evenly curved to avoid a wavy waistline.

steps.title: Finish the Petersham Ends
steps.content: Trim excess Petersham above the zipper. Fold the ends neatly back and tuck them inside the waistband edge. Hand stitch the folded ends and secure them to the side seams.

tip.text: Hand stitching creates a cleaner finish than machine stitching in this area.

steps.title: Hem the Skirt
steps.content: Neaten the lower edge of the skirt. Press up a 1.6 inch hem and hand stitch in place to complete the garment.

tip.text: Ensure the hem depth remains consistent all the way around the skirt.

items.name: Neck Rest & Travel Bag
items.finishedSize: Neck rest: 10.63 inches x 14.17 inches, Make-up bag: 6.69 inches x 9.06 inches
items.materialNeeded: Assorted cottons, fat quarters. Plain cotton scraps, mint, yellow, green. Pom pom trim, teal, yellow, pale pink. Fusible interfacing. Yellow zip, 9.84 inches. Polystyrene bead filling. Dried lavender.
items.techniques: Machine Embroidery, Hand Stitching, Zipper Insertion, Topstitching

steps.title: Travel Pillow - Cut fabric pieces
steps.content: Download the neck rest template. Cut two pieces from coordinating fabric, flipping the template for the second half. Repeat the same process for the back of the cushion.

tip.text: Mirror the second template piece accurately so both sides of the neck rest remain symmetrical after assembly.

steps.title: Travel Pillow - Sew front and back pairs
steps.content: Pin each front and back pair right sides together. Stitch with a 0.2 inches seam across the short straight edge. Press the seams open and set aside.

tip.text: Press seam allowances open after stitching to reduce bulk and create a smoother finished shape.

steps.title: Travel Pillow - Cut sleep pocket pieces
steps.content: For the yellow sleep pocket, cut two pieces from plain yellow fabric using the template.

tip.text: Use a removable fabric marker to trace pocket outlines before cutting for greater accuracy.

steps.title: Travel Pillow - Sew sleep pocket
steps.content: Pin the two yellow pieces together. Stitch with a 0.2 inches seam across the straight edge. Press the seam open.

tip.text: Keep seam allowances consistent on small pocket pieces to prevent mismatched edges after folding.

steps.title: Travel Pillow - Fold and topstitch pocket
steps.content: Fold back wrong sides together. Topstitch across the seam near the edge.

tip.text: Edge topstitching not only decorates the pocket but also helps it hold its shape during use.

steps.title: Travel Pillow - Embroider 'sleep' text
steps.content: Trace the word 'sleep' onto white tissue paper. Pin it in place on the pocket. Machine or hand embroider over the letters. Tear the tissue away carefully. Iron on the reverse side.

tip.text: Remove the tissue paper gently after stitching to avoid loosening the embroidery threads.

steps.title: Travel Pillow - Clip curved edges
steps.content: Carefully make 0.2 inches snips all the way around the curved edge of the pocket. Iron the tabs onto the wrong side.

tip.text: Clip close to the curve without cutting into the seam allowance or the pocket edge may weaken.

steps.title: Travel Pillow - Attach sleep pocket
steps.content: Pin the pocket in place on the neck rest. Machine stitch around the curve just inside the edge, leaving the top open.

tip.text: Avoid stretching the curved pocket edge while sewing or it may ripple after stitching.

steps.title: Travel Pillow - Make faux pocket
steps.content: Cut one piece of mint fabric. Apply interfacing to the back. Stitch the word 'repeat' onto it. Stitch all around the edges to attach.

tip.text: Fuse the interfacing before embroidery to stabilize the fabric and improve stitch quality.

steps.title: Travel Pillow - Attach pom pom trim to front
steps.content: Pin teal pom pom trim right sides together around the right side of the black half. Start at the join and work around until reaching the curved end. Stitch in place.

tip.text: Keep pom pom trim facing inward while stitching so the decorative edge appears correctly after turning.

steps.title: Travel Pillow - Attach pom pom trim to back
steps.content: Repeat the process with yellow pom pom trim for the teal half of the neck rest.

tip.text: Match the trim ends carefully where they meet to avoid visible gaps in the finished border.

steps.title: Travel Pillow - Sew front and back together
steps.content: Pin the finished front and back pieces right sides together. Leave a 1.97 inches gap for turning. Stitch with a 0.2 inches seam allowance.

tip.text: Do not forget to leave the turning gap or the neck rest cannot be turned right side out.

steps.title: Travel Pillow - Snip curves and turn out
steps.content: Snip the curved edges carefully. Turn the pillow right side out through the gap.

tip.text: Clip curves at regular intervals to help the neck rest lie smoothly after turning.

steps.title: Travel Pillow - Fill with beads
steps.content: Make a funnel from paper. Fill the pillow with polystyrene beads. Hand stitch the gap closed.

tip.text: Fill gradually and test the firmness often because overfilling can make the neck rest uncomfortable.

steps.title: Make-up Bag - Cut lining pieces
steps.content: Using the make-up bag template, cut two pieces from plain lining fabric. Fuse interfacing to both pieces.

tip.text: Apply interfacing before cutting if the lining fabric is lightweight and prone to shifting.

steps.title: Make-up Bag - Cut back piece
steps.content: Cut one more piece from lining fabric for the back of the bag. Set all three lining pieces aside.

tip.text: Label similar-looking pieces immediately after cutting to avoid assembly confusion later.

steps.title: Make-up Bag - Cut and stitch front panels
steps.content: Trim the three pieces for the bag front. Stitch them together with a 0.2 inches seam. Press the seams open.

tip.text: Press each seam open before attaching decorative elements to keep the front panel flat.

steps.title: Make-up Bag - Cut palm tree shapes
steps.content: Back a piece of plain dark green fabric with interfacing. Use the template to draw two palm tree tops on the reverse side. Cut them out.

tip.text: Trace applique shapes onto the interfacing side to keep visible fabric surfaces clean.

steps.title: Make-up Bag - Cut tree trunks
steps.content: Back a fabric print with interfacing. Cut two tree trunks and trim.

tip.text: Cut both tree trunks together to ensure they remain identical in size and shape.

steps.title: Make-up Bag - Attach palm tree to bag front
steps.content: Pin the palm tree tops and trunks in place on the bag front. Machine stitch around the edges twice. Iron on the reverse side.

tip.text: Use a shorter stitch length around applique curves to achieve smoother outlines.

steps.title: Make-up Bag - Cut zip tabs
steps.content: Cut two tab ends for the zip, measuring 1.46 inches x 4.41 inches. Turn the short ends in by 0.2 inches. Press.

tip.text: Press the tab folds sharply before stitching to create neat and durable zip ends.

steps.title: Make-up Bag - Fold zip tabs
steps.content: Fold each tab in half, bringing the short ends together. Press again.

tip.text: Uneven folds can cause the zip tabs to sit crooked and affect bag alignment.

steps.title: Make-up Bag - Prepare zip
steps.content: Slide the zip pull into the middle of the zip. Trim the metal ends from both sides so the zip measures 8.35 inches.

tip.text: Move the zip pull away from the cutting area before trimming the metal ends.

steps.title: Make-up Bag - Attach tabs to zip ends
steps.content: Insert each end of the zip into the short open end of a fabric tab by 0.24 inches. Stitch across close to the fold edge several times to secure.

tip.text: Backstitch several times across the tabs because these areas experience frequent stress.

steps.title: Make-up Bag - Attach zip to bag front and lining
steps.content: Lay the zip centrally, wrong side up, onto the top of the bag front (right side up). The top edge of the zip should be level with the raw edge. Lay a lining piece wrong side up on top. All edges should match, with the zip in the middle. Pin along the top. Use a zip foot to stitch. Trim away the tab overhang so all edges align.

tip.text: Using a regular presser foot can make it difficult to stitch close enough to the zip teeth.

steps.title: Make-up Bag - Attach zip to bag back and second lining
steps.content: Fold everything back so the front of the zip and bag are facing you. Place the zip face down onto the right side of the remaining outer piece (bag back). Lay the last lining piece on top, right side down. Open the bag flat (right side up) and press both sides close to the zip. Topstitch along both sides of the zip.

tip.text: Topstitching near the zip helps prevent the lining from catching when the bag is opened.

steps.title: Make-up Bag - Open zip and align pieces
steps.content: Open the zip completely. Align all the right sides together. Push the edge of the zip towards the lining. Match the topstitching seams. Pin in place. Repeat at the other end. Pin around all the raw edges.

tip.text: Always open the zip before sewing the bag closed or turning the bag later will be difficult.

steps.title: Make-up Bag - Stitch around bag edges
steps.content: Machine stitch a 0.2 inches seam all the way around the raw edges of the bag. Leave the corner angles unstitched. Leave a 2.95 inches gap in one side of the lining. Keep the bag laid flat.

tip.text: Leave the lining opening large enough to comfortably pull the bag through without stressing seams.

steps.title: Make-up Bag - Stitch boxed corners left side
steps.content: Pinch the corners of the left side of the bag together so the seams match. Pin and stitch a 0.2 inches seam. Repeat on the other corner of the same side.

tip.text: Match the side and bottom seams precisely when boxing corners to create an even base.

steps.title: Make-up Bag - Stitch boxed corners right side
steps.content: Repeat the boxed corner process for the two corners on the right-hand side of the bag.

tip.text: Measure both boxed corners before stitching to ensure the bag stands evenly.

steps.title: Make-up Bag - Turn bag right side out
steps.content: Pull the bag through the gap in the lining to turn it right side out.

tip.text: Turn the bag slowly through the lining opening to avoid stressing the zip seams.

steps.title: Make-up Bag - Close lining gap and finish
steps.content: Stitch the gap in the lining closed. Push the lining inside the bag. Press well.

tip.text: Push the lining corners fully into place before pressing to achieve a crisp finished shape.

items.name: Rainbow Patchwork Sew-Along: Part Two - Cushion & Bag
items.finishedSize: Travel pillow: approximately 11.81 inches x 9.84 inches, Drawstring backpack: approximately 15.75 inches x 12.60 inches
items.materialNeeded: Plain cottons in assorted pastel rainbow shades; plain white cotton; pastel coloured heavyweight cotton. Cotton cord, 0.28 inches. Fibre filling. Eyelets, 0.47 inches. Quilt wadding. Tape for aglets.
items.techniques: Patchwork, Curved piecing, Quilting, Topstitching, Channel sewing, Eyelet fitting

steps.title: Travel Pillow - Cut and mark arch pieces for rainbow
steps.content: Cut arch number one from white cotton and mark the guide points on the edge of the seam allowance. Cut arch number two from coloured cotton, then mark guide points on both outer and inner edges of the curve. Clip the inner curve of piece two to 0.08 inches from the traced seam line.

tip.text: Clip only up to the seam allowance and never into the stitching line, or weak spots may appear along the rainbow curves.

steps.title: Travel Pillow - Sew inner curve and add arches three to five
steps.content: Pin, tack and sew the inside edge of piece two to the outside edge of piece two, matching the guide points and easing the fabric around the curve without overly stretching it. Open it out and press the seam allowance towards piece two. Cut arch numbers three, four, and five. Stitch to the outer curve of the work in turn, using the method detailed above.

tip.text: Pulling the fabric while sewing curved seams can distort the arches and create puckers that are difficult to remove.

steps.title: Travel Pillow - Add arch six, press and pin to wadding
steps.content: Precise mark matching and tacking will result in smooth curves each time. Cut piece number six from white cotton but extend the vertical sides of piece six by 1.18 inches. Stitch to the outside of the rainbow in the same way as before. Press the whole block well and pin onto quilt wadding.

tip.text: Press each completed arch before adding the next one to keep the rainbow curves smooth and accurately shaped.

steps.title: Travel Pillow - Quilt, trim and sew pillow edges
steps.content: Quilt through the layers around each curve of the rainbow. Trim the outside edge of the rectangular block into a curve to match the rainbow, if a shaped pillow is desired. Lay the block face down onto plain cotton the same size and sew around the edges, leaving a small gap in the side for stuffing.

tip.text: Uneven quilting lines can distract from the rainbow design, so use the seam lines as visual guides while stitching.

steps.title: Travel Pillow - Turn, stuff and close pillow
steps.content: Turn out and push out the corners of the pillow well before filling. Use small pieces of fibre filling bit by bit to achieve a smooth pillow, pushing it well into the corners and filling to a medium squishiness. Turn in the raw edges of the gap and slip-stitch closed.

tip.text: Add stuffing gradually in small amounts to achieve an even shape without creating lumps inside the pillow.

steps.title: Drawtring Backpack - Make quilt block and topstitch panel
steps.content: Make a quilt block as detailed for the pillow but this time, use coloured cotton for pieces one and six. Trim the bottom of the block with a 0.98 inches x 12.60 inches strip the same colour. Place face down onto plain cotton, 9.45 inches x 12.60 inches, and sew all the way round, leaving a small gap in the bottom edge. Turn out through the gap and press the raw edges to the inside. Topstitch across the top of the panel only.

tip.text: Push the seam allowances fully inside the turning gap before topstitching or the panel edge may appear uneven.

steps.title: Drawtring Backpack - Cut main pieces, attach panel and sew sides
steps.content: Cut two pieces of heavyweight cotton, 17.32 inches square, and bind the edges with a wide zig zag to prevent excessive fraying. Pin the panel to the centre of one piece, 2.17 inches up from the bottom edge and topstitch around the three sides to make a large pocket. Place the square panel right sides together and sew down the sides and across the bottom, leaving 3.94 inches unstitched at the top of each side. Turn out.

tip.text: Measure the pocket placement from both side edges before stitching because an off-centre pocket is very noticeable on the finished backpack.

steps.title: Drawtring Backpack - Create top channel and fit eyelets
steps.content: Fold under the seam allowance of the unstitched areas at the top of the bag and topstitch, sewing across the top of each side seam as you do so to strengthen it. Fold in 0.2 inches, then 1.18 inches along the top edges of the bag. Sew close to the edge of the hem to make a wide channel. Stitch diagonally across the bottom corners of the bag and fit a 0.47 inches eyelet into the centre of each resulting triangle, passing through both layers of the corners.

tip.text: Backstitch several times around the channel openings and eyelets since these areas experience the most wear during use.

steps.title: Drawtring Backpack - Thread cords and tie reef knot
steps.content: Take two 51.18 inches lengths of cotton cord and bind the ends with tape to make aglets. Pass one cord through the top of the bag channels from the left front, around the gap and return to the left back. Pass the other cord from the right front, around through the gap and return to the right back. Thread the ends of the cords through the corresponding eyelets at the bottom of the back and tie in a neat reef knot.

tip.text: Wrap the cord ends tightly with tape before threading to create temporary aglets that slide easily through channels and eyelets.

items.name: Positive Pants Card and Brooch
items.finishedSize:
Card: 7.09 x 7.09 inches 
Keyring: 3.35 inches diameter
Brooch: 3.35 inches diameter
items.techniques:
Appliqué
Machine Sewing
Hand Embroidery
Decorative Stitching
Tissue Paper Transfer
Fabric Layering
Topstitching
Gathering Lace
Gluing
Keyring Construction
Brooch Making
items.materialNeeded:
Fabrics: plain turquoise, orange, yellow, peach; patterned: caramel ditsy floral, white check, navy star, navy letter; faux leather in silver
Lace in cream
Lightweight iron-on interfacing
Felt in silver glitter
Pom pom trim in aqua
Split ring
Greeting card blank, 7.09 inches
Acid-free glue stick
Brooch back
Ribbons: turquoise chevron, teal grosgrain

steps.title: Make a Card - Cover card front and stitch pants back
steps.content: Download and print the templates. Using an acid-free glue stick, cover the front of the card blank in turquoise, allowing for a 1 inch overlap over the spine onto the back. Cut a 4.92 inches diameter circle from caramel ditsy floral, back with iron-on interfacing, then stitch it directly onto the card front using orange thread. Using the template provided, snip out the back of the pants from plain orange and the front of the pants from peach floral. Back both pieces with interfacing, then stitch the back of the pants onto the caramel floral circle.

tip.text: Small applique pieces can distort while stitching if they are not stabilized with interfacing before sewing.

steps.title: Make a Card - Embroider text and stitch pants front
steps.content: Trace the words onto white tissue. Secure the tissue in place, sew over the words twice in navy thread, then tear the tissue away. Stitch lengths of cotton lace trim across the top and around the legs, tucking the raw ends over onto the reverse. Using a coordinating thread, stitch the finished pant front in place on the card. Trace the 'you can do this' onto tissue, secure in place across the bottom of the card, leaving space for the pom-pom trim. Sew over it twice in navy thread.

tip.text: Pull the tissue paper away gently after stitching to avoid loosening or distorting the embroidered lettering.

steps.title: Make a Card - Attach pom-pom trim and felt stars
steps.content: Use acid-free glue to secure a length of aqua pom-pom trim around the caramel floral circle, making sure the ends meet at the top left of the pants. Cut out five felt stars from glittery silver. Position, then sew in place.

tip.text: Lay out all embellishments before attaching them permanently to achieve a balanced decorative composition.

steps.title: Stitch a keyring - Create keyring base and stitch shorts
steps.content: Download and print the templates. Cut a plain yellow 3.35 inches circle and use acid-free glue to stick it to the middle of the slightly larger leatherette circle. Sew once around the yellow circle using teal thread. Use the templates to cut out the boxer shorts from navy letter print and the middle section from star print. Stick them in place in the middle of the circle with acid-free glue. Secure the edges with bright orange thread. Sew a piece of chevron stripe ribbon across the top of the shorts. Use the tissue method to stitch 'think positive' and 'lucky pants'.

tip.text: Check the applique placement from multiple angles before stitching because off-centre designs are difficult to correct later.

steps.title: Stitch a keyring - Assemble keyring with ribbon loop and split ring
steps.content: Sew a length of turquoise ribbon down the middle of a length of wider chevron ribbon. Trim the excess leatherette with pinking shears close to the inner circle. Place it right side up onto a second piece of wrong side up leatherette. Sew over the original line of stitching to secure, inserting the raw ends of the ribbon loop you have made with a split ring in place into the top. Trim to finish.

tip.text: Backstitch securely where the ribbon loop is inserted, as this area carries most of the stress during use.

steps.title: Sew a brooch - Prepare brooch base and stitch pants with lace
steps.content: Download and print the templates. Use pinking shears to cut out a 3.35 inches diameter circle from turquoise square print. Use bright orange thread to stitch it to a slightly larger circle of silver faux leather. Cut out the back of the pants from plain peach and the front of the pants from caramel floral. Stick them in the middle of the circle. Take a length of frilly lace and gather it so it fits across the top of the pants, sew to secure.

tip.text: Distribute the lace gathers evenly across the waistband area to avoid bulky sections and create a cleaner finish.

steps.title: Sew a brooch - Finish brooch with text, star and brooch back
steps.content: Using the tissue paper method, use navy thread to sew the words in place. Place it right side up onto the wrong side of a circle of silver faux leather. Sew around the original line of bright orange stitching, then trim off the excess to leave a small border of silver. Stitch a small silver glitter star in place and attach a brooch back to finish.

tip.text: Trim the faux leather close to the stitching line, but leave enough margin to prevent the seam from pulling apart.

items.name: Kids’ Bumblebee Rucksack
items.finishedSize:
Backpack: 17.32 inches x 17.32 inches
Purse: Approximately 4.72 inches x 3.15 inches excluding frame
items.techniques:
Patchwork
Pocket Construction
Topstitching
Zip Installation
Quilting
Bag Construction
Strap Making
Stay Stitching
Gusset Construction
Snap Fastener Installation
Purse Frame Installation
Fabric Gluing
Hand Sewing
Slip Stitching
Turning and Pressing
items.materialNeeded:
Plain cotton: black, yellow, pale fabric
Patterned cotton: assorted prints (alternating black and yellow)
Lining fabric: plain cotton
Lightweight quilt wadding
Zip
Snap fasteners (plier fix type)
Purse frame
Fabric glue
Polyester thread (matching colours)

steps.title: Back Pack - Cut and layer pocket rectangles
steps.content: Download the templates, then print and cut out. Cut two rectangles of patterned and plain cotton to make the front and back of the backpack. Using the template, trim the top corners into a gentle curve. Cut 11.02 inches wide rectangles of other prints, alternating between black and yellow and making the largest 17.32 inches long, then 13.39 inches, 9.45 inches and 5.51 inches. Fold each of these rectangles in half, right sides out, and topstitch along the fold.

tip.text: Press each folded pocket edge before topstitching to create crisp openings that stay flat during use.

steps.title: Back Pack - Topstitch pocket sections
steps.content: Layer the rectangles together, matching up the bottom edges starting with the second smallest and second largest. Topstitch down the centre to make two pocket sections. Layer the smallest rectangle over these and topstitch two vertical lines through the top two layers only to make three pocket sections. Layer the pieces onto the largest rectangle and the front panel of the bag. Zig zag stitch around the sides and bottom edge to hold them in place.

tip.text: Skipping temporary basting can allow the pocket layers to shift, resulting in uneven compartment widths.

steps.title: Back Pack - Make and attach wings
steps.content: Trace two wing templates onto right sides together pale fabric and layer onto lightweight quilt wadding. Sew along the traced line, leaving the straight edge unstitched, before cutting out the wings with a 0.20 inches seam allowance and clipping the angle. Turn the wings out and press well. Place a wing on either side of the pocketed front panel, matching up the raw edges, and stay stitch in place.

tip.text: Trim the seam allowance at wing points carefully without cutting into the stitching line or holes may appear after turning.

steps.title: Back Pack - Sew zip gusset
steps.content: Cut two pieces of patterned and lining material, 1.97 inches x 19.69 inches. Place one plain and one patterned gusset piece right sides together and sandwich one edge of the zip between these, teeth facing the printed fabric and matching the edges. Stitch down the edge 0.20 inches from the teeth, turn the fabrics out so they lay next to the zip and topstitch 0.28 inches from the teeth. Repeat this action with the other two pieces of fabric on the other edge of the zip. Bind the raw edges of the gusset together with a zig zag stitch.

tip.text: If the zip teeth are caught in the seam allowance, the zipper may become difficult to open and close smoothly.

steps.title: Back Pack - Attach zip gusset ends
steps.content: Snip two pieces of both patterned and plain fabric, 3.94 inches x 8.27 inches. Place one patterned and one plain piece right sides together and sandwich one end of the zip gusset between the two, matching up the raw edges. Sew across the short edge, taking care to not stitch through any metal parts. Turn the fabrics out and topstitch the seam before matching up the remaining raw edges and binding together with a zig zag stitch. Repeat for the other end of the zip gusset.

tip.text: Hand-turn the machine wheel when sewing near zip stops to avoid broken needles or damaged hardware.

steps.title: Back Pack - Make shoulder straps
steps.content: Cut two strips of fabric, 3.15 inches x 17.72 inches and two strips of wadding, 1.18 inches x 17.72 inches. Fold each strip of fabric in half lengthways right sides together and stitch down the long seam. Turn out and press so the seam lays in the centre. Use a safety pin to thread the wadding strips into each tube, keeping it flat. Topstitch down both long sides of each strap to hold the wadding in place. Stay-stitch each strap to the top and bottom of the patterned bag back, matching raw edges.

tip.text: Use a safety pin to guide the wadding through the strap evenly and prevent twisting inside the tube.

steps.title: Back Pack - Sew gusset to bag front and lining
steps.content: Lay the pocketed bag front face up and pin one edge of the face down zip gusset around the top and sides, starting at the centre of each piece and working out in both directions. When happy with the fit, lay the bag lining piece over the top of the gusset and re-pin. Sew around the top and sides before turning out and check the seam encloses all the raw edges. Pin and stitch the back of the bag to the other edge of the gusset in the same way, unzipping the bag first to make it easier and taking care when stitching the seam to avoid other parts of the bag getting caught up.

tip.text: Always leave the zipper partially open before sewing the final seams so the backpack can be turned right side out.

steps.title: Back Pack - Sew bottom gusset and lining
steps.content: Cut a rectangle of patterned and a rectangle of plain fabric, 4.33 inches x 11.02 inches. Fold in 0.20 inches on one long edge of the lining piece and lightly glue or tack. Turn the unzipped backpack inside out, sandwich the bottom front of the bag between the unhemmed long sides of the two rectangles, right sides together and matching the raw edges. Stitch along this seam leaving 0.39 inches unsewn at each end. Manipulate the fabric to sandwich one side of the bag between one short edge of the fabric pieces and sew.

tip.text: Misaligned gusset corners can make the backpack base uneven and affect how the bag stands.

steps.title: Back Pack - Finish bottom and add snap fasteners
steps.content: Repeat for the remaining short side. Flip the lining fabric so the seams are enclosed. Sew the remaining long bottom edge of the front of the bag to the patterned fabric only before pinning and oversewing the hemmed edge of the lining over this raw seam. Turn the backpack out. Fit plier fix snap fasteners to the centres of the larger pockets if desired.

tip.text: Test the pocket flap position before installing snaps to ensure the closures meet evenly.

steps.title: Purse - Make striped panel and strap
steps.content: Cut three strips of yellow and three strips of black fabric, 1.18 inches x 4.72 inches. Sew together to make a striped panel and press. Layer onto quilt wadding and topstitch along the seams. Using the template, snip one shape from the striped panel, one from a patterned fabric and two from lining material, marking the hinge position. Cut a strip of fabric, 1.18 inches x 3.15 inches. Fold in 0.20 inches on both long edges, then fold in half lengthways and topstitch to make a strap. Fold the strap in half and tack to one side of the striped front body, matching the raw edges so the loop sits on the bee.

tip.text: Inconsistent seam allowances between the black and yellow strips can make the finished bee pattern look uneven.

steps.title: Purse - Sew flaps and lining
steps.content: Place the patterned and striped fabric and linings right sides together and sew around the top section from hinge position to hinge position. Turn out the resulting flaps and press. Match up the flaps right sides together and sew the patterned and striped pieces together around the curve of the purse from the ends of the previous seams, securing the strap as you do so. Fold the lining pieces up, tuck the flaps out of the way inside the purse, and sew the linings together, leaving a 1.57 inches gap in the bottom edge. Fold in and glue the edges of the gap before turning the purse right sides out. Slip-stitch the gap in the lining closed before tucking it inside the purse and pressing well.

tip.text: Do not stitch the lining opening closed until the purse has been fully turned and checked for shape.

steps.title: Purse - Attach purse frame with glue
steps.content: Check the fit of the flaps inside the channels of the purse frame. Apply fabric glue into the channel on one side of the frame and tuck the edges of one flap into it, using a knitting needle if necessary and adjusting before the glue sets. Once this side is completely dry, fit the other flap in the same way.

tip.text: Allow the first side of the purse frame to dry completely before gluing the second side to prevent misalignment.

steps.title: Purse - Make and attach wings
steps.content: Trace out wing template onto right sides together pale fabric and layer it onto lightweight quilt wadding. Sew along the drawn line leaving a 1.18 inches gap in the lower edge. Cut out the wings leaving a 0.20 inches seam allowance and turn out. Fold in the raw edges of the gap and slip-stitch closed before pressing well. Fix the wings to the centre of the striped body with a few hand stitches.

tip.text: Use a blunt turning tool to shape the wing curves neatly before pressing and attaching them.

items.name: Peacock Clutch Bag
items.techniques:

items.materialNeeded:
Plain cotton, 9.84 inches x 15.75 inches
Patterned fabric, 9.84 inches x 15.75 inches
Contrast cotton, 15.75 inches x 17.72 inches
Patterned fabric scraps
Quilt wadding, 9.84 inches x 15.75 inches
Embroidery thread
Zip, 9.84 inches
Seam allowance: 0.24 inches throughout unless stated otherwise

steps.title: Make a Card - Sew petals together
steps.content: Download the pattern. Using the petal template, cut nine shapes from patterned fabric. Fold each one in half lengthways with the printed side innermost, and stitch across the top diagonal edge. Turn right side out, ease out the point and press. Lay out the petals in the correct colour order. Pin the first two together along the long edges, with right sides together.

tip.text: Use a point turner or blunt knitting needle to gently shape each petal tip without piercing the fabric.

steps.title: Make a Card - Applique lower centre piece
steps.content: Sew from the bottom to top, leaving the top 0.24 inches unstitched, and securing the seam with a few back stitches. Press the seam open and add the other petals in the same way. Pin the lower centre template to orange fabric and cut out 0.24 inches from the edge. Fold this margin over, along the curved edge and tack down over the paper. Press, then remove the template. Pin centrally over the raw edges at the centre of the applique.

tip.text: Skipping seam pressing between petals can cause the feather applique to become bulky and uneven.

steps.title: Make a Card - Assemble bag layers and hand stitch applique
steps.content: Using the bag template, cut a front, back, two linings and two pieces of wadding. Pin and tack the applique centrally to the bag front, lining up the bottom edges. Sandwich the bag front, a piece of wadding and a lining piece together. Tack round the outside edge. Hand stitch down the outline of the applique, sewing through all the layers. Layer a back, wadding and lining for the other side, and tack as before.

tip.text: Do not stretch the wadding while basting, or the quilted panel may become distorted after stitching.

steps.title: Stitch a keyring - Sew zip and assemble bag body
steps.content: Open out a zip and with right sides facing, sew one side of the tape to the front and the other to the back, between the points marked A. With right sides together, pin the front and back together along the side and bottom edges. Machine stitch, then refold the bottom corners so that points B align and stitch across the seam. Bind the seams with tape and turn through.

tip.text: Many beginners forget to open the zip before sewing the bag closed, making it difficult to turn the project right side out.

steps.title: Stitch a keyring - Make tassel for zip pull
steps.content: Make a small tassel for the zip pull by sewing a loop of different embroidery threads through the hole and binding them together at the top. Trim the ends to finish.

tip.text: Wrap the tassel neck tightly several times before knotting to keep the strands secure during everyday use.

items.name: Fat Quarter Easy Make Picnic Project Bundle
items.finishedSize:
Straw Pouch: 2.36 x 18.90 inches
Frilled Pouch: Approx. 8.66 x 5.91 inches

items.materialNeeded:
Printed cotton fabric
Plain cotton fabric
Contrasting cotton fabric
Quilt wadding
Snap fasteners
7.48 inches zip
Matching sewing thread
items.techniques:
Machine Sewing
Topstitching
Zip Installation
Patchwork
Quilting
Pleating
Bag Construction
Binding
Boxed Corners
Snap Fastener Installation
Turning and Pressing

steps.title: Straw Pouch
steps.content: Cut two coordinating fabric strips measuring 2.36 x 18.90 inches and place them right sides together. Stitch across both short ends using a narrow seam allowance. Sew down 2.36 inches along both long edges from one short end to create the pouch flap section.

tip.text: Mark the flap stitching points on both sides before sewing to ensure the pouch flap is symmetrical.

steps.title: Form the Pouch Structure
steps.content: Open the unstitched section and align the bottom seam with the end of the flap stitching, matching the same fabrics right sides together. Sew from the flap to the fold along both sides of the outer fabric. Repeat with the lining fabric, leaving a 1.18 inches turning gap in one side.

tip.text: Do not accidentally sew across the opening in the lining or the pouch cannot be turned right side out.

steps.title: Turn and Press the Pouch
steps.content: Turn the pouch right side out through the opening in the lining. Fold the raw edges of the gap inward and topstitch it closed before pushing the lining back inside. Press the pouch carefully, especially around the flap edge.

tip.text: A well-pressed flap creates sharper edges and helps the pouch close neatly.

steps.title: Install Snap Fasteners
steps.content: Fold the flap into position and create small holes through the centre of the flap and the matching location on the pouch front. Install coordinating snap fasteners using pliers to complete the closure.

tip.text: Test the flap position before installing snaps because incorrect placement can prevent the pouch from closing properly.

steps.title: Frilled Pouch - Cut Fabric Pieces and Create Frill
steps.content: Cut two lining pieces, two wadding pieces and one outer pouch piece using the template. Cut a second outer piece into upper and lower sections, adding seam allowance along the dividing line. Cut a contrasting strip measuring 2.76 x 19.69 inches, fold it lengthwise with right sides facing out and press. Pleat the strip evenly and pin it along the top edge of the lower outer section.

tip.text: Uneven pleats can cause the frill to look unbalanced across the front of the pouch.

steps.title: Join Outer Sections and Add Wadding
steps.content: Place the upper outer section over the frill with right sides together and stitch along the seam. Fold the pieces open, press the seam toward the upper section and topstitch. Layer the completed outer panels onto the wadding and secure around the edges with zigzag stitching.

tip.text: Topstitching helps keep the frill lying flat and prevents the seam from rolling forward.

steps.title: Insert the Zipper
steps.content: Place the zipper face down along the top edge of one outer panel and position a lining piece over it. Stitch close to the zipper teeth, fold the layers open and topstitch. Repeat on the opposite side of the zipper with the remaining outer and lining pieces.

tip.text: Keep the stitching line close to the zip teeth without catching them to ensure smooth operation.

steps.title: Assemble and Box the Corners
steps.content: Open the zipper halfway and place the pouch pieces right sides together. Stitch around the sides and bottom edge. Trim and finish the seams, then fold the cut corners in the opposite direction and stitch across the opening to create a boxed base. Finish the seams and turn the pouch right side out through the zipper opening.

tip.text: Open the zip halfway before sewing the pouch closed or turning the pouch later will be difficult.

steps.title: Picnic Blanket - Cut and Arrange Patchwork Pieces
steps.content: Cut multiple 9.06 inches cotton squares from a mixture of printed and plain fabrics. Use the template guide to divide the squares into sections. Mix pieces from different fabrics and sew them back together in reverse order to create unique patchwork blocks.

tip.text: Distribute colours and prints throughout the blanket to create a balanced patchwork design.

steps.title: Assemble the Patchwork Top
steps.content: Press each completed block and trim to 8.27 inches square. Arrange the blocks into a four-by-four layout, keeping the orientation consistent and avoiding identical fabrics touching. Sew the blocks together to create the blanket top.

tip.text: Carefully align seam intersections when joining blocks to avoid uneven patchwork rows.

steps.title: Quilt the Layers
steps.content: Layer the patchwork top over quilt wadding and a plain backing fabric. Pin all layers securely and topstitch along the major seam lines to quilt the blanket. Trim away excess wadding and backing fabric.

tip.text: Pin thoroughly before quilting so the patchwork, wadding and backing remain aligned.

steps.title: Bind and Finish the Edges
steps.content: Join several fabric strips into one continuous binding strip. Attach the binding to the back of the quilt, mitring the corners neatly. Fold the binding over to the front, tuck the raw edge under and topstitch close to the folded edge to finish the blanket.

tip.text: Incorrectly folded corners can create bulky binding and affect the finished appearance of the blanket.

items.name: Drawstring Cotton Fish Bags
items.materialNeeded:
Printed cotton fat quarters
White lining fabric
Medium-weight quilt wadding
Buttons
Matching sewing thread
items.techniques:
Machine Sewing
Topstitching
Bag Construction
Lining Construction
Turning and Pressing
Channel Construction
Drawstring Making
Slip Stitching
Appliqué Attachment

steps.title: Fish Bag - Cut and Sew the Fins and Tail
steps.content: Download and print the templates. Cut two body pieces and two top pieces from coordinating cotton fabrics. Cut two fin pieces and one tail piece from fabric folded right sides together. Layer the fin and tail pieces with quilt wadding and sew around the curved edges, leaving the straight edges open. Clip the curves, turn right side out and press flat.

tip.text: Clip seam allowances carefully around curves without cutting into the stitching to help the fins and tail turn smoothly.

steps.title: Fish Bag - Attach Fins and Prepare Body Panels
steps.content: Position a fin at the top centre of each body piece and baste in place. Sew a top section to each body piece with right sides together along the straight edge. Press the seam open and topstitch close to the seam line. Cut two lining pieces to match the completed body panels.

tip.text: Topstitching after pressing helps keep the seam flat and adds structure to the top of the bag.

steps.title: Fish Bag - Assemble Outer Bag and Lining
steps.content: Join each lining piece to the top edge of a body panel, sewing across the top and partially down each side. Clip the corners and turn the top flaps right side out. Baste the tail to the bottom of one body panel. Place the body panels right sides together, align all seams and sew around the perimeter, leaving a turning gap in the lining. Turn the bag right side out through the opening.

tip.text: Misaligned flap seams can cause uneven drawstring channels, so match the seams carefully before stitching.

steps.title: Fish Bag - Create the Drawstring Channels
steps.content: Close the turning gap with slip stitches and push the lining into the bag. Fold the top flaps down by half their depth and topstitch to form two drawstring channels around the bag opening.

tip.text: Press the flap folds before topstitching to create straighter and more consistent channels.

steps.title: Fish Bag - Sew Fabric Drawstrings
steps.content: Cut two fabric strips measuring 1.18 x 11.81 inches. Fold each strip lengthwise and stitch from the centre opening toward each end, tapering the stitching near the tips. Turn the tubes right side out through the centre opening.

tip.text: Leave the turning opening accessible in the middle of the tube or the drawstring cannot be turned right side out.

steps.title: Fish Bag - Insert Drawstrings and Add Eyes
steps.content: Slip-stitch the drawstring openings closed and press the tubes. Thread one drawstring through each channel and tie the ends together with neat reef knots. Sew buttons onto both sides of the fish to create the eyes and complete the project.

tip.text: Check both drawstrings gather evenly before tying the knots permanently.

items.name: Knitting Project Storage Bag
items.materialNeeded:
Printed cotton fabric
Coordinating plain cotton fabric
Medium-weight quilt wadding
Stiff card insert
Buttons
Matching sewing thread
items.techniques:
Machine Sewing
Topstitching
Bag Construction
Lining Construction
Quilting
Box Construction
Slip Stitching
Turning and Pressing
Edge Finishing

steps.title: Project Bag - Prepare Handle and Side Panels
steps.content: Download and print the templates. Cut the basket panels from assorted printed cotton fabrics. Join the handle tops using the extension pieces and topstitch the seams to strengthen them. Attach the front side panels to each side of the handle panels to create an H-shaped basket body. Press the completed pieces onto quilt wadding, trim the wadding to match and secure around the edges with zigzag stitching.

tip.text: Topstitching the handle extension seams helps reinforce areas that will receive the most stress during use.

steps.title: Project Bag - Add Lining and Form Basket Sides
steps.content: Place each wadded basket piece onto coordinating lining fabric with right sides together and trim the lining to match.
